questions.question: Which best describes Java dependency identification compared to NuGet?
choices: Java uses a single package ID and version, identical to NuGet.
Java uses three-part coordinates: groupId:artifactId:version, globally unique on Maven Central.
Java uses a URL plus version, so every dependency points at a specific server.
Java has no central repository; dependencies are vendored per project.
questions.explanation: Java dependencies use groupId:artifactId:version coordinates, e.g. org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-web:3.2.0. NuGet uses a single ID plus version and relies on author naming conventions; Java's three-part identity is enforced by Maven Central.

questions.question: You run 'mvn package' on a Spring Boot project. Which describes what executes?
choices: Only the package phase runs; previous phases must be invoked manually.
Every Maven lifecycle phase up to and including package - validate, compile, test, then package.
Only compilation; tests are excluded unless mvn test is invoked separately.
An arbitrary set of tasks Maven chooses based on the project's build.gradle file.
questions.explanation: Maven's lifecycle is cumulative: invoking 'mvn package' runs validate, compile, test, then package in order. Tests run by default unless explicitly skipped with -DskipTests.

questions.question: Why does a Spring Boot project usually inherit from spring-boot-starter-parent or import its BOM?
choices: It's required by Maven to build any Spring Boot app.
It pins curated versions for all spring-boot-starter-* dependencies and configures plugins, so you don't manage versions by hand.
It enables the Spring Boot dashboard in IntelliJ.
It auto-generates application.yml from the dependencies.
questions.explanation: The parent (or its BOM equivalent) provides curated, version-aligned dependency declarations and plugin configuration. You add a dependency without a version, and the parent picks one that's tested against the Boot version you're using.
